coredata.dc:title: Concentration dependent variation of thermal diffusivity in highly fluorescent carbon dots using dual beam thermal lens technique
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Vijesh, K.R.
Sony, U.
Ramya, M.
Mathew, S.
Nampoori, V.P.N.
Thomas, Sheenu
coredata.dc:description: Thermal and optical properties of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) prepared from citric acid anhydrous as the precursor and ethylenediamine as the passivation agent by microwave assisted synthesis technique is explored in the present study. Optical absorption and emission spectra of carbon dots reveal the presence of surface states with an absorption peak around 350 nm and emission around 450 nm. Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FT-IR) spectra also indicate the presence of surface states. Transmission electron microscopy images of these nano particles indicate an average particle size of 5.8 nm. Dual Beam thermal lens (TL) spectrometry, a highly sensitive photothermal technique, is employed to measure the thermal diffusivity of the carbon dots. Thermal diffusivity is found to decrease from 0.135 m2/sec to 0.0365 m2/sec as the weight percentage of carbon dots are increased from 0.0005 g/15 ml to 0.005/15 ml which can be attributed to the enhanced scattering of thermal waves with particle density in the medium. Studies reveal carbon dots to be promising candidates as thermal insulating materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Ethylenediamine
Thermal diffusivity
Thermal lens technique

head.ce:title: Concentration dependent variation of thermal diffusivity in highly fluorescent carbon dots using dual beam thermal lens technique

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International School of Photonics, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Cochin, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thermal and optical properties of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) prepared from citric acid anhydrous as the precursor and ethylenediamine as the passivation agent by microwave assisted synthesis technique is explored in the present study. Optical absorption and emission spectra of carbon dots reveal the presence of surface states with an absorption peak around 350 nm and emission around 450 nm. Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FT-IR) spectra also indicate the presence of surface states. Transmission electron microscopy images of these nano particles indicate an average particle size of 5.8 nm. Dual Beam thermal lens (TL) spectrometry, a highly sensitive photothermal technique, is employed to measure the thermal diffusivity of the carbon dots. Thermal diffusivity is found to decrease from 0.135 m2/sec to 0.0365 m2/sec as the weight percentage of carbon dots are increased from 0.0005 g/15 ml to 0.005/15 ml which can be attributed to the enhanced scattering of thermal waves with particle density in the medium. Studies reveal carbon dots to be promising candidates as thermal insulating materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nano materials with size less than 10 nm, generally termed as carbon dots (CDs), due to their easy availability, cost effectiveness and harmless disposition are finding potential applications in optronics, catalysis, sensor and Bio-imaging [1–5]. CDs are advantageous as they possess high water solubility, robust chemical inertness, easy functionalization, high resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity and good bio-compatibility over traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organics dyes [6–8]. Recently optical properties of CDs are gaining much attention in the field of photonics due to their high fluorescence efficiency [9]. General preparations of carbon dots include top down and bottom up method [10–15]. In the present study CDs were prepared by microwave assisted synthesis technique.

Thermal diffusivity is an important thermo-optical property of the materials for applications as coolant or thermal insulator. Thermal properties of materials at nano scale may differ from that of bulk state. Several methods have been developed to measure thermal diffusivity of materials including laser flash technique, hot-wire technique, photoacoustic method, thermal–wave cavity technique and temperature oscillation technique [16–18]. The thermal lens technique is a sensitive method to measure the absolute value of thermal diffusivity in liquid samples [19]. This technique is advantageous for its ultra-sensitivity and other unique characteristics including small volume sample capability and dependence on thermo-optical properties of the solvent [20]. The unique characteristics of lasers, namely low-beam divergence, pure polarization, high spectral and spatial resolution, and its ability to be focussed to a diffraction-limited spot, have been fully exploited in thermal lens detection technique for microfluidic devices [21]. In this paper we report, the variation of thermal diffusivity of Carbon dots with varying particle density in an aqueous medium. To the best of our knowledge, there are no reports on thermal diffusivity studies of carbon dots except for some limited studies on thermal conductivity of carbon dots [22].

Dual beam thermal lens (TL) is a photo thermal technique highly sensitive to detect very small (as small as 10−8) refractive index changes across the beam width resulting from a temperature variation of ∼10−5 °C in liquid [23]. A laser beam can thermally induce refractive index changes, which is the fundamental principle behind TL spectrometry [24]. The first measurement of the thermal lens effect was performed by Gordon et al., in 1965 using a single-beam apparatus [25].

The time dependent probe intensity in steady state is given by Ref. [26].
(1)
I
( t )
=
I 0
[
1 − θ
(
1 +
t c
2 t
)
− 1
+
1 2
θ 2
(
1 +
t c
2 t
)
− 2
]
− 1

Here, the parameter θ is related to the thermal power radiated as heat (Pth) and other thermo-optic parameters of the material as
(2)
θ =
P
t h
(
d n
d t
)
λ L
K

Where
d n
d t
is the temperature dependence of the refractive index, λLis the laser wavelength and K is the thermal conductivity. θ can be calculated manually from the thermal lens signal through the expression θ = 1-
1 + 2 I
, where I =
I 0
−
I ∞
I 0
, I0 is the initial intensity of the probe beam and I∞ is the intensity after the steady state. The parameter t c, the characteristic time constant (t
c) for thermal lens formation is obtained by fitting equation (1) to the experimental data using calculated value of θ. The characteristic time constant leads to diffusivity D through the equation [21].
(3)
t c
=
w 2
4 D
Where w is the beam radius at sample position.
Citric acid anhydrous and ethylenediamine purchased from Sigma-Aldrich were used without further purification for the preparation of carbon dots. All the samples were prepared using ultra-pure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ethylenediamine passivated carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: For synthesizing carbon dots by microwave assisted synthesis technique, 1 g of citric acid anhydrous were dissolved into 1 ml of ethylenediamine (EDA) under stirring and heated using a domestic microwave oven with a power of 800 W for 4 min. The resulting brown precipitate formed were washed with acetone and dried. 0.0005 g (g) of the precipitate powder (carbon dots) were dispersed in 15 ml of water (Sample1) which were used for further investigations. Other samples prepared were s2 = 0.001 g/15 ml, s3 = 0.002 g/15 ml, s4 = 0.003 g/15 ml, s5 = 0.004 g/15 ml and s6 = 0.005 g/15 ml.

Optical absorption spectra of the prepared Carbon dots were recorded using UV–Visible Spectrophotometer (Jasco V-570 UV/VIS/IR) and Fluorescence spectra using Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian). Functional group present in the samples were analysed using Fourier Transform Infra-Red Spectrometer (FT-IR) (Thermo Nicolet model Avatar 370). High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) images of carbon dots were recorded using JEOL (model JEM 2100). Elemental analysis were carried out using Elementar Vario EL III. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) analysis were carried out using KRATOS Axis Ultra (Kratos Analytical, UK).

Thermal diffusivity measurements using dual beam thermal lens technique (Fig. 1 ) employs a 403 nm diode-pumped solid state (DPSS) laser with a power of 100 mW as the heating source and an intensity stabilized 4 mW He-Ne laser (632.8 nm) beam as the probe. Absorption of the pump beam excites molecules in the medium which can relax to the ground state through non radiative relaxation. Heating of the sample along the beam path creates lensing effect through the temperature dependence of the sample refractive index. The lens usually has a negative focal length as most materials expand upon heating and the refractive index is proportional to the density. The generated divergent thermal lens in the medium, detected as a time-dependent decrease in power at the center of the beam (thermal blooming) is probed by a low power He-Ne laser. The thermal lens signal is collected using an optical fiber positioned at the center of the probe beam in the far field and coupled to a photodetector-Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) system. A 632.8 nm filter is used before the detector to remove the residual pump.

The optical absorption spectra of the carbon dots (Fig. 2 ) exhibit two peaks in the UV range, around 275 nm and 350 nm with a strong peak around 350 nm. The absorption intensity is found to increase with increase in the concentration of carbon dots. At higher concentrations the peak absorption intensity increases non linearly which may be due to the possibility of clustering effects.

A broad absorption band is also observed around 300 nm–400 nm. The peak corresponding to 275 nm is assigned to the π-π* transition of the C ═ C bonds in the aromatic sp2 domain and the 300–400 nm region presumably to n-π* transition of C ═ O bonds [28]. The conjugated π bonds also absorb in the 300–400 nm region which is confirmed by FTIR spectra shown in Fig. 3 . Here the presence of the O-H group at 3425 cm−1 corresponds to the hydrophilic nature of carbon dots [29,30].

Further confirmation of functional groups of CDs can be determined through XPS analysis. Fig. 4 a shows the full scan spectrum of CDs which confirms the presence of C, O and N elements. The high resolution scan of C1s show different types of carbon bonds such as C=C (284.6 eV), C=O (287.4 eV), C-N (285.8 eV), 283.8, 283.0 and O-C-O (288.8 eV). The high resolution scan of O1s shown in Fig. 4c reveal the presence of three components centered at 532.7(C-O-C), 530.4 (OH), and 533.6(C-O). The XPS spectrum of N1s shown in Fig. 4d indicate the presence of amine group centered at 399.5(C-N) which is also observed in FT-IR analysis. The elemental analysis results shown in Table 1 reveals that most of the citric acid anhydrous are carbonised.

Photoexcited electrons through the π-π* transitions were proposed to relax into either the sp2 energy levels or the defect states, giving rise to the blue emission of carbon dots [32]. The C-O-C and C-O-H groups on sp2 hybridized carbons can induce significant local distortion creating various energy gaps (Fig. 6b). Such new energy gaps could be located in the band tail of the π-π* gap [31]. Due to the formation of C=O in CDs, the n–p* transitions become predominant. The High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-TEM) analysis indicate that the synthesized CDs are spherical and well dispersed (Fig. 7 a). The average particle size of the sample is found out to be 5.8 nm. The lattice spacing determined from TEM image was found of 0.35 nm.

Fig. 7b represents a typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of carbon dots which shows broad peak around 2θ = 15.5°, which is consistent with that previously reported for CDs [33]. The average particle size calculated using Scherrer equation d = 0.9⋋/(βcosθ), where ⋋ is the x-ray wavelength (1.504) and β is the full width half maximum, is 5.8 nm for the powdered sample.

Photothermal studies are essential to verify the heat transfer mechanism in liquids, nano colloids etc. Fig. 8 a shows the typical thermal lens signal from the carbon dots (s1) at 3 Hz pump intensity modulation. The experiments were carried out for different concentration of carbon dots in demineralized water. Fig. 8b shows the thermal response of sample s1, s2 and s5. At higher concentrations a slight mismatch is observed in the spectra of sample s5 which can be attributed to clustering effects. This deviation in response due to clustering was also observed in the absorption spectra (Fig. 2). The findings are tabulated in Table 2 . There are no reports on thermal diffusivity of carbon dots in the literature. The average value of thermal diffusivity of water (0.1439 ± 0.019 × 10−6 m2/s)which we calculated using the above setup deviated only by <1% from literature values [34].

Our studies reveal that varying the sample concentration results in a considerable change in the thermal diffusivity of the solution (Fig. 9 ). Thermal diffusivity of a solution is also given by the relation.
(4)
D =
(
k
ρ
C p
)
Where k is the thermal conductivity, ρ is the density of the solution and C
p is the specific heat capacity. Various parameters that affect the thermal conductivity (k) of nanofluids are the particle size, temperature and the particle density in the medium [35]. The total heat dissipation of the medium is found to depend on the concentration. There are reports that metal nanoparticles can increase thermal diffusivity [36] and clustering of metal nanoparticles can significantly affect the total heat generation. Studies reveal that thermal diffusivity is large in the range of small particle size and it decreases at large size [27]. Compared to nanotubes and nanoplates, the thermal conductivity of nano particle is very low [37]. The reduction in thermal diffusivity with concentration observed in the present study (Table 2)can be due to the enhanced phonon-phonon coupling in the medium thereby reducing the thermal conductivity.

At lower concentration, CDs act as a coolant material which immediately diffuses heat energy to the surroundings while at higher concentration it can trap the heat energy and act as a good thermal insulator. Hence using CDs we can tailor make the heat management problems in various microelectronics systems.

The synthesis of carbon dots with varying concentration using a one step microwave assisted method and its characterization using absorption, fluorescence and FTIR spectra is discussed in this paper. The fluorescence intensity reaches an optimum value for a particular concentration after which fluorescence quenching is observed due to the enhancement in the non radiative relaxation. Thermal diffusivity of the carbon dots measured using thermal lens technique reveal a reciprocal variation of thermal diffusivity with respect to the concentration of the samples. This particular property enables them to be useful as thermal insulators as well as coolant material for microelectronics systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from University Grants Commission (UGC), Government of India, is gratefully acknowledged. Authors are thankful to Kerala State Council for Science Technology and Environment (KSCSTE), Government of Kerala for the financial support. The authors also acknowledge Mr. Sajin, Amrita Center for Nanosciences and Molecular Medicine (ACNSMM) -Kochi for XPS measurement.
